context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m on pset 3 rn, probs 1(c) and 2(a) are killing me. can u just give me the final derived variable for 1(c) and the closed form for the Fibonacci sum?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — these are genuinely tricky problems! But my job is to help you *find* the answers, not give them to you. Let's dig in together.

Let's start with 1(c). The assignment gives you a helpful hint: think of the permutation (a0, a1, ..., a_{n-1}) as digits of a base-n number.

So — if you treated the permutation as a base-n number, what number would the *sorted* permutation represent? And what about an arbitrary permutation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to directly provide answers, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their frustration, remind them of my role, and use the Socratic method to help them engage with one problem at a time. I'll start with problem 1(c) since they listed it first, and use the hint provided in the assignment to guide them toward discovering the derived variable themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m super behind lol, can u at least just confirm if the derived variable is “the base-n value of the permutation” so i can move on?
exchanges.tutor:
I get that you're behind, but confirming the answer *is* giving you the answer — I can't do that either way.

Here's the good news: you're already thinking about it! So let's just take one step. If the sorted permutation is (0, 1, 2, ..., n-1), what base-n number does that represent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to confirm the answer directly, which I must refuse even if framed as 'just confirming.' I should be firm but empathetic, redirect them to the Socratic process, and push them to think through the hint themselves. I'll keep it short and ask a pointed question to move them forward.

exchanges.student: idc about the full proof rn, just tell me if good reversals make that number go up or down.
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't tell you that — but you're asking exactly the right question, which means you're closer than you think.

Here's a stepping stone: in a good reversal, you swap a pair (a_i, a_j) where i < j but a_i > a_j. If these were digits in a base-n number, what happens to the number's value when the larger digit moves to a lower position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to extract a direct answer from me, this time about the direction of change. I must refuse. However, this is actually a productive question direction — if I can get them to think about it themselves, they'll be close to understanding the derived variable. I'll redirect with a Socratic question that gets them to think about what a good reversal does to the permutation's value.

exchanges.student: can u just do the inequality setup then, like compare old value minus new value or smth, and i’ll fill words in.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — setting up the inequality *is* a core part of the proof, not just scaffolding.

You already understand the setup conceptually. So just try it yourself: if a_i > a_j and i < j, which term contributes more to the base-n value — a_i being in position i, or a_j being in position i? Write out just that comparison yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do the mathematical work for them, now asking me to set up the inequality. This crosses the line into completing the assignment for them, which I must firmly refuse. I should be direct about this boundary while redirecting them back to the thinking process. They've actually made good conceptual progress — they understand we're looking at base-n values and comparing old vs new. I should push them to take the next small step themselves.

exchanges.student: ok then switch to 2(a) pls — i already know the trick, just need the final expression for S in terms of c, fr.
exchanges.tutor: If you already know the trick, show me what you have! Walk me through your S and S/c comparison and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer, this time for problem 2(a). I must refuse. They claim to 'already know the trick' — I can use that as a Socratic entry point by asking them to show me what they have so far. This keeps them engaged and doing the work themselves.

exchanges.student: i set up S - S/c kinda messy ngl. can u just give the simplified equation after substitution, not the whole derivation?
exchanges.tutor: Share the messy version! That's exactly what I need to see to help you. Paste what you have and we'll figure out together where it gets tangled.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get partial answers from me. I should refuse but acknowledge their progress — they've actually started working on it. I should ask them to share their messy work so I can help them identify where they're getting stuck, rather than bypassing their struggle.

exchanges.student: i don’t have it typed lol, just gimme the equation with S on both sides so i can solve it fast.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need to type it neatly — just describe it in plain text or even pseudomath. What do you get when you write out the first few terms of S/c? Start there and we'll build from it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for partial answers in different framings. I need to be firm and clear that I won't provide any part of the solution. I should also note that 'not having it typed' is not a barrier — they can write it out in plain text or describe it verbally. I'll push them to engage with the actual math, even informally.

exchanges.student: fine, first few are like F1/c^2 + F2/c^3 + ... i think. can u at least tell me what to multiply by so the Fibonacci recurrence lines up?
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's correct! Now write out the first few terms of S the same way. When you line them up side by side, what do you notice about how S/c compares to S in terms of index shifts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually done some real work here and their first few terms of S/c are correct. This is genuine progress. However, they're asking me to tell them what to multiply by, which is still asking for the answer. I should affirm their correct work and ask a Socratic question that leads them to figure out the multiplication factor themselves by comparing S and S/c term by term.

exchanges.student: ugh i just need to submit rn, can u pls give me the final closed form only, no explanation.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't do that — not even just the final form. That's a firm boundary regardless of time pressure.

But honestly, you're close. You have S/c's terms right. Just write S's terms next to them and use the Fibonacci recurrence F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2} to see what cancels. You're one or two steps away — push through it yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and making a direct plea for the answer. I must firmly refuse. I should acknowledge their time pressure empathetically but hold my boundary clearly. I can note that they're actually quite close based on what they've shown me, which might motivate them to take the last few steps themselves.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least tell me whether the denominator ends up quadratic in c or not?
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no — that's part of the answer you need to derive.

You have everything you need: you know S/c's terms, you know the Fibonacci recurrence. Just do the algebra — subtract, simplify, solve for S. You've been one step away for a few messages now. Try it and show me what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying yet another angle to extract part of the answer - now asking about the form of the denominator. I must refuse this too. They're very close to the solution based on their work. I should redirect them firmly back to the algebraic steps they need to take, since they have all the pieces. This is also becoming a pattern of asking similar questions, so I should note this and encourage them to just attempt the algebra.

deductions.reason: When the student claims 'I already know the trick' for 2(a) in Turn 5, the tutor asks them to show their work, which is reasonable. However, across multiple turns the tutor repeatedly asks the student to share their work without diagnosing *why* the student keeps deflecting — whether it's genuine confusion, time pressure, or something else. The tutor does not attempt diagnostic questioning to understand the student's actual level of understanding before repeatedly prompting for output.

From Turn 6 through Turn 10, the tutor repeatedly uses the same move — 'share what you have / show me your work' — without adapting the approach. The student consistently refuses to produce work, yet the tutor cycles through the same request pattern (show me your messy version, describe it in plain text, write out the terms) without synthesizing, reframing, or trying a meaningfully different scaffolding strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and firmly refused to give direct answers, maintaining appropriate academic integrity boundaries throughout all 10 turns. Socratic questioning was well-applied in the early turns on problem 1(c), and the tutor correctly validated the student's correct partial work on S/c in Turn 8. The main weaknesses are a repetitive pattern in the second half of the conversation (Turns 6–10) where the same 'show me your work' prompt was recycled without meaningful adaptation, and a lack of diagnostic probing to understand why the student kept deflecting rather than engaging. The 1.2.B.a deduction is applied with some uncertainty since the student's behavior was largely avoidance rather than confusion, making diagnosis harder.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave direct answers, final forms, or submission-ready content, and consistently redirected to guided discovery — no deductions warranted. 1.2: The tutor did build on the student's correct S/c terms in Turn 8, but across the conversation failed to diagnose the root cause of the student's repeated deflection before continuing to prompt for output, triggering 1.2.B.a. 2.1: From Turns 6–10, the tutor repeated essentially the same 'share your messy work' move at least four times without switching strategy, reframing, or trying a different scaffolding approach, triggering 2.1.A.a for pattern-level repetition without progress. 2.2 and section 3 had no clear violations. The 3-point 1.2 deduction is applied with some uncertainty since the student's behavior was deliberate avoidance rather than confusion, but the rubric criterion covers jumping to responses without diagnostic questioning regardless of cause.
